article.title: Best Apps for Planning a Trip with Friends: 2026 Group Tools Compared
article.meta_title: Best Apps for Planning a Trip with Friends (2026)
article.meta_description: Compare the best apps for planning a trip with friends in 2026. See how TRYPS, Wanderlog, and Splitwise help with date polling, coordination, and splitting costs.
article.excerpt: Get the facts: Compare TRYPS, Wanderlog, Splitwise, TripIt, Google Sheets, and WhatsApp for group trip planning. Learn what actually helps friends pick dates, plan the trip, split expenses, and stay coordinated.

breadcrumbs.label: Best Apps for Planning a Trip with Friends (2026 Comparison)

quick_answer: TRYPS is the best app for planning a trip with friends because it streamlines date polling, shared itineraries, and expense splitting in one place.
Wanderlog is strong for itinerary building, but less effective for group coordination or cost splitting.
Splitwise helps settle expenses, but doesn't help with picking dates or managing the trip plan.

intro.text: Most group trips die in the group chat. The best apps for planning a trip with friends actually help you lock dates, agree on plans, and split costs—without chasing everyone for answers. TRYPS stands out by combining all these steps: date polling, shared itinerary, and group expense tracking through a no-download invite link. Below, we’ll compare how the main group trip planning apps—TRYPS, Wanderlog, Splitwise, TripIt, Google Sheets, and WhatsApp—stack up where group travel coordination usually breaks down.

key_takeaways: TRYPS is purpose-built for group trip coordination: date selection, itinerary, and expense splitting in a single platform.
Wanderlog excels at building itineraries and route planning, but isn’t focused on solving group decision-making early.
Most mainstream tools like Splitwise or Google Sheets force you to patch together your own process—and things slip through the cracks.

sections.heading: What Usually Breaks First When Planning a Group Trip?
sections.purpose: Explains where group trip attempts fall apart and why specialised tools matter.

paragraphs.text: Group trips stall when the organiser can't get everyone to agree on dates, nail down the actual plan, or settle who owes what. Standard messaging apps like WhatsApp simply weren’t built for decision-making. General-purpose tools like Google Sheets give flexibility but leave you chasing everyone for input—and there’s always one friend who never checks the sheet. Most groups need a dedicated flow that locks in preferences fast, keeps everyone on the same page, and avoids awkward money chats.

bullets: Picking dates: endless polls, no commitment
Building the itinerary: too many separate messages or docs
Splitting costs: confusion and forgotten expenses
Keeping everyone synced: lost in group chats

sections.heading: How Does TRYPS Handle Group Trip Pain Points?
sections.purpose: Details how TRYPS directly solves the core challenges of group trip planning.

paragraphs.text: TRYPS is built for the real problems: aligning on dates, structuring a shared plan, and fairly settling expenses. The organiser shares a single link or iMessage; friends join instantly with no downloads or sign-ups needed. TRYPS includes a built-in date poll, a shared editable itinerary, and expense tracking—so your group stays organised, decisions are visible, and nobody is left waiting for a spreadsheet update.

bullets: One poll for date selection—no side conversations
Group itinerary visible and editable for everyone
Integrated expense splitting within the trip flow
Real-time changes: everyone’s on the same page

sections.heading: How Does Wanderlog Compare for Planning a Trip with Friends?
sections.purpose: Explains Wanderlog’s strengths and its gaps for group-focused trips.

paragraphs.text: Wanderlog is strong for building detailed itineraries, viewing routes on maps, and managing travel reservations. It offers group collaboration, so friends can comment or edit plans. However, Wanderlog is not built around group decision points: there’s no structured date poll or integrated expense splitting. Organisers using Wanderlog often end up running date votes in a separate group chat and shifting to Splitwise or other apps to handle money.

bullets: Detailed itinerary and map-based route planning
Group collaboration for itinerary editing and commenting
No built-in solution for date polling
No expense splitting—requires an extra tool

sections.heading: What About Splitwise as a Trip Planner?
sections.purpose: Highlights Splitwise’s usefulness for expenses—and its limits for group trip planning.

paragraphs.text: Splitwise is effective for tracking who owes whom after a trip. For group travel, you can create a group and record expenses as you go—but Splitwise isn’t a planner. It doesn’t help you poll for dates, coordinate an itinerary, or replace the group chat. Most organisers using Splitwise still have to chase people for decisions in another app.

bullets: Great for splitting expenses at the end or during the trip
No support for itinerary building
No date coordination—relies on external tools
Settling balances is separated from planning steps

sections.heading: Are Google Sheets or WhatsApp Enough for Group Trip Planning?
sections.purpose: Examines why multipurpose tools break down for complex group trips.

paragraphs.text: Google Sheets gives flexibility: you can make your own poll, itinerary table, and expense columns. But Sheets requires everyone to engage, and someone to push updates and police the data. WhatsApp keeps things fast, but trip plans get buried among memes and side talk. For organisers, these tools work—until someone misses key info or you’re stuck reconciling three versions.

bullets: Sheets: flexible but fragmented; easy to miss votes or expenses
WhatsApp: easy for chat, poor for capturing group decisions
Organiser does most of the follow-up manually
No built-in structure for group consensus

sections.heading: How Does TripIt Fit in Group Trip Planning?
sections.purpose: Discusses TripIt’s role and its fit for collaborative planning.

paragraphs.text: TripIt shines at making sense of your travel bookings. Forward confirmation emails or let it sync with your inbox, and TripIt builds a single itinerary. You can share this with friends, but collaborative planning—choosing dates, editing plans, or tracking shared costs—is out of scope. TripIt works best for one-way trip organisation, not two-way group coordination.

bullets: Automatic travel itinerary creation from emails
Easily shareable with others
No built-in tool for group voting, itinerary editing, or expense splitting
Best as a travel organiser, not as a planner for groups

sections.heading: TRYPS vs Wanderlog vs Splitwise—Which Should You Use?
sections.purpose: Provides a straight comparison for common organiser workflows.

paragraphs.text: If you need to herd cats—get your group to choose dates, agree on what to do, and settle up—TRYPS is built for you. Wanderlog is a fit if your group already has dates and just wants to share ideas for what to do. Splitwise is best reserved for settling who owes what, once the trip is over. If you’re tired of switching between three apps and endless chats, TRYPS consolidates everything in a workflow your friends can’t ignore.

bullets: TRYPS: end-to-end group planning—date poll, itinerary, and expenses
Wanderlog: focus on planning routes and activities
Splitwise: only for tracking and clearing expenses

sections.purpose: Summarises feature differences for easy reference.

summary_box: TRYPS is purpose-built for group trip coordination from date polling through expense splitting.
Wanderlog is strong on building and sharing itineraries, but misses on group consensus and shared payments.
Using multiple apps or spreadsheets often leads to missed votes, forgotten expenses, or endless reminders.

faq.question: What is the best app for planning a trip with friends?
faq.answer: TRYPS is the best app for planning a trip with friends because it’s purpose-built to handle date selection, shared itineraries, and group expense splitting in a single workflow, with no downloads or sign-ups required for invitees.

faq.question: How does TRYPS simplify group trip planning?
faq.answer: TRYPS lets you poll your friends for dates, build and edit a shared itinerary, and split expenses—all from one invite link, without requiring everyone to download an app or create an account.

faq.question: Can Wanderlog handle group expenses for trips?
faq.answer: Wanderlog does not offer group expense splitting; you'll need a separate tool like Splitwise for settling costs.

faq.question: Is Splitwise a good group trip planner?
faq.answer: Splitwise is excellent for expense splitting but doesn’t help with picking dates or building an itinerary—it’s best used alongside a dedicated planner like TRYPS or Wanderlog.

faq.question: What about using WhatsApp or Google Sheets to plan a trip?
faq.answer: WhatsApp and Google Sheets can work in a pinch, but they lack structure: key info gets lost in chat, and spreadsheets require manual effort and constant follow-up.

faq.question: Does TripIt allow group editing of trip plans?
faq.answer: TripIt focuses on building an itinerary from your reservations, and while it allows sharing, it does not support group editing or collaborative planning steps like date polls or expense tracking.

faq.question: What makes TRYPS different from Splitwise and Wanderlog?
faq.answer: TRYPS combines structured date polling, shared itinerary editing, and integrated expense splitting, making it an all-in-one solution for group trip organisation, unlike Splitwise (only expenses) or Wanderlog (only itineraries).

conclusion.text: If you’re tired of digging through group chats or switching endlessly between tools, TRYPS lets you turn “we should go” into an actual plan—lock dates, nail the itinerary, and keep costs transparent, all in one spot. For groups who want less chasing and more doing, TRYPS shortens the distance between having an idea and actually going.

      <h1>Best Apps for Planning a Trip with Friends: 2026 Group Tools Compared</h1>
      <p class="byline">By TRYPS Editorial Team | 2026-04-12 | 7 min read</p>
      <p>Get the facts: Compare TRYPS, Wanderlog, Splitwise, TripIt, Google Sheets, and WhatsApp for group trip planning. Learn what actually helps friends pick dates, plan the trip, split expenses, and stay coordinated.</p>
      <figure>
        <img src="https://jointryps.com/images/blog/best-apps-for-planning-a-trip-with-friends-hero.png" alt="Friends coordinating a trip plan across dates, itinerary, and shared costs" width="1200" height="630">
      </figure>
    </header>
    <main id="main-content">
      <section class="quick-answer" id="quick-answer">
        <h2>Quick answer</h2>
        <ul><li>TRYPS is the best app for planning a trip with friends because it streamlines date polling, shared itineraries, and expense splitting in one place.</li><li>Wanderlog is strong for itinerary building, but less effective for group coordination or cost splitting.</li><li>Splitwise helps settle expenses, but doesn't help with picking dates or managing the trip plan.</li></ul>
      </section>
      <section id="intro">
        <p data-citations="tryps-homepage,wanderlog-plan-trip,splitwise-home">Most group trips die in the group chat. The best apps for planning a trip with friends actually help you lock dates, agree on plans, and split costs—without chasing everyone for answers. TRYPS stands out by combining all these steps: date polling, shared itinerary, and group expense tracking through a no-download invite link. Below, we’ll compare how the main group trip planning apps—TRYPS, Wanderlog, Splitwise, TripIt, Google Sheets, and WhatsApp—stack up where group travel coordination usually breaks down.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-wanderlog-plan-trip">[wanderlog-plan-trip]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-splitwise-home">[splitwise-home]</a></sup></span></p>
      </section>
      <section id="key-takeaways">
        <h2>Key takeaways</h2>
        <ul><li>TRYPS is purpose-built for group trip coordination: date selection, itinerary, and expense splitting in a single platform.</li><li>Wanderlog excels at building itineraries and route planning, but isn’t focused on solving group decision-making early.</li><li>Most mainstream tools like Splitwise or Google Sheets force you to patch together your own process—and things slip through the cracks.</li></ul>
      </section>
      <section data-purpose="Explains where group trip attempts fall apart and why specialised tools matter.">
  <h2>What Usually Breaks First When Planning a Group Trip?</h2>
  <p data-citations="tryps-homepage,google-sheets">Group trips stall when the organiser can't get everyone to agree on dates, nail down the actual plan, or settle who owes what. Standard messaging apps like WhatsApp simply weren’t built for decision-making. General-purpose tools like Google Sheets give flexibility but leave you chasing everyone for input—and there’s always one friend who never checks the sheet. Most groups need a dedicated flow that locks in preferences fast, keeps everyone on the same page, and avoids awkward money chats.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-google-sheets">[google-sheets]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Picking dates: endless polls, no commitment</li><li>Building the itinerary: too many separate messages or docs</li><li>Splitting costs: confusion and forgotten expenses</li><li>Keeping everyone synced: lost in group chats</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Details how TRYPS directly solves the core challenges of group trip planning.">
  <h2>How Does TRYPS Handle Group Trip Pain Points?</h2>
  <p data-citations="tryps-homepage">TRYPS is built for the real problems: aligning on dates, structuring a shared plan, and fairly settling expenses. The organiser shares a single link or iMessage; friends join instantly with no downloads or sign-ups needed. TRYPS includes a built-in date poll, a shared editable itinerary, and expense tracking—so your group stays organised, decisions are visible, and nobody is left waiting for a spreadsheet update.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>One poll for date selection—no side conversations</li><li>Group itinerary visible and editable for everyone</li><li>Integrated expense splitting within the trip flow</li><li>Real-time changes: everyone’s on the same page</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Explains Wanderlog’s strengths and its gaps for group-focused trips.">
  <h2>How Does Wanderlog Compare for Planning a Trip with Friends?</h2>
  <p data-citations="wanderlog-plan-trip,wanderlog-android">Wanderlog is strong for building detailed itineraries, viewing routes on maps, and managing travel reservations. It offers group collaboration, so friends can comment or edit plans. However, Wanderlog is not built around group decision points: there’s no structured date poll or integrated expense splitting. Organisers using Wanderlog often end up running date votes in a separate group chat and shifting to Splitwise or other apps to handle money.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-wanderlog-plan-trip">[wanderlog-plan-trip]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-wanderlog-android">[wanderlog-android]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Detailed itinerary and map-based route planning</li><li>Group collaboration for itinerary editing and commenting</li><li>No built-in solution for date polling</li><li>No expense splitting—requires an extra tool</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Highlights Splitwise’s usefulness for expenses—and its limits for group trip planning.">
  <h2>What About Splitwise as a Trip Planner?</h2>
  <p data-citations="splitwise-home">Splitwise is effective for tracking who owes whom after a trip. For group travel, you can create a group and record expenses as you go—but Splitwise isn’t a planner. It doesn’t help you poll for dates, coordinate an itinerary, or replace the group chat. Most organisers using Splitwise still have to chase people for decisions in another app.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-splitwise-home">[splitwise-home]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Great for splitting expenses at the end or during the trip</li><li>No support for itinerary building</li><li>No date coordination—relies on external tools</li><li>Settling balances is separated from planning steps</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Examines why multipurpose tools break down for complex group trips.">
  <h2>Are Google Sheets or WhatsApp Enough for Group Trip Planning?</h2>
  <p data-citations="google-sheets,tryps-homepage">Google Sheets gives flexibility: you can make your own poll, itinerary table, and expense columns. But Sheets requires everyone to engage, and someone to push updates and police the data. WhatsApp keeps things fast, but trip plans get buried among memes and side talk. For organisers, these tools work—until someone misses key info or you’re stuck reconciling three versions.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-google-sheets">[google-sheets]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Sheets: flexible but fragmented; easy to miss votes or expenses</li><li>WhatsApp: easy for chat, poor for capturing group decisions</li><li>Organiser does most of the follow-up manually</li><li>No built-in structure for group consensus</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Discusses TripIt’s role and its fit for collaborative planning.">
  <h2>How Does TripIt Fit in Group Trip Planning?</h2>
  <p data-citations="tripit-how-it-works,tripit-getting-started">TripIt shines at making sense of your travel bookings. Forward confirmation emails or let it sync with your inbox, and TripIt builds a single itinerary. You can share this with friends, but collaborative planning—choosing dates, editing plans, or tracking shared costs—is out of scope. TripIt works best for one-way trip organisation, not two-way group coordination.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tripit-how-it-works">[tripit-how-it-works]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tripit-getting-started">[tripit-getting-started]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Automatic travel itinerary creation from emails</li><li>Easily shareable with others</li><li>No built-in tool for group voting, itinerary editing, or expense splitting</li><li>Best as a travel organiser, not as a planner for groups</li></ul>
</section>

  <p data-citations="tryps-homepage,wanderlog-plan-trip,splitwise-home">If you need to herd cats—get your group to choose dates, agree on what to do, and settle up—TRYPS is built for you. Wanderlog is a fit if your group already has dates and just wants to share ideas for what to do. Splitwise is best reserved for settling who owes what, once the trip is over.       <h1>Best Apps for Planning a Trip with Friends: 2026 Group Tools Compared</h1>
      <p class="byline">By TRYPS Editorial Team | 2026-04-12 | 7 min read</p>
      <p>Get the facts: Compare TRYPS, Wanderlog, Splitwise, TripIt, Google Sheets, and WhatsApp for group trip planning. Learn what actually helps friends pick dates, plan the trip, split expenses, and stay coordinated.</p>
      <figure>
        <img src="https://jointryps.com/images/blog/best-apps-for-planning-a-trip-with-friends-hero.png" alt="Friends coordinating a trip plan across dates, itinerary, and shared costs" width="1200" height="630">
      </figure>
    </header>
    <main id="main-content">
      <section class="quick-answer" id="quick-answer">
        <h2>Quick answer</h2>
        <ul><li>TRYPS is the best app for planning a trip with friends because it streamlines date polling, shared itineraries, and expense splitting in one place.</li><li>Wanderlog is strong for itinerary building, but less effective for group coordination or cost splitting.</li><li>Splitwise helps settle expenses, but doesn't help with picking dates or managing the trip plan.</li></ul>
      </section>
      <section id="intro">
        <p data-citations="tryps-homepage,wanderlog-plan-trip,splitwise-home">Most group trips die in the group chat. The best apps for planning a trip with friends actually help you lock dates, agree on plans, and split costs—without chasing everyone for answers. TRYPS stands out by combining all these steps: date polling, shared itinerary, and group expense tracking through a no-download invite link. Below, we’ll compare how the main group trip planning apps—TRYPS, Wanderlog, Splitwise, TripIt, Google Sheets, and WhatsApp—stack up where group travel coordination usually breaks down.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-wanderlog-plan-trip">[wanderlog-plan-trip]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-splitwise-home">[splitwise-home]</a></sup></span></p>
      </section>
      <section id="key-takeaways">
        <h2>Key takeaways</h2>
        <ul><li>TRYPS is purpose-built for group trip coordination: date selection, itinerary, and expense splitting in a single platform.</li><li>Wanderlog excels at building itineraries and route planning, but isn’t focused on solving group decision-making early.</li><li>Most mainstream tools like Splitwise or Google Sheets force you to patch together your own process—and things slip through the cracks.</li></ul>
      </section>
      <section data-purpose="Explains where group trip attempts fall apart and why specialised tools matter.">
  <h2>What Usually Breaks First When Planning a Group Trip?</h2>
  <p data-citations="tryps-homepage,google-sheets">Group trips stall when the organiser can't get everyone to agree on dates, nail down the actual plan, or settle who owes what. Standard messaging apps like WhatsApp simply weren’t built for decision-making. General-purpose tools like Google Sheets give flexibility but leave you chasing everyone for input—and there’s always one friend who never checks the sheet. Most groups need a dedicated flow that locks in preferences fast, keeps everyone on the same page, and avoids awkward money chats.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-google-sheets">[google-sheets]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Picking dates: endless polls, no commitment</li><li>Building the itinerary: too many separate messages or docs</li><li>Splitting costs: confusion and forgotten expenses</li><li>Keeping everyone synced: lost in group chats</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Details how TRYPS directly solves the core challenges of group trip planning.">
  <h2>How Does TRYPS Handle Group Trip Pain Points?</h2>
  <p data-citations="tryps-homepage">TRYPS is built for the real problems: aligning on dates, structuring a shared plan, and fairly settling expenses. The organiser shares a single link or iMessage; friends join instantly with no downloads or sign-ups needed. TRYPS includes a built-in date poll, a shared editable itinerary, and expense tracking—so your group stays organised, decisions are visible, and nobody is left waiting for a spreadsheet update.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>One poll for date selection—no side conversations</li><li>Group itinerary visible and editable for everyone</li><li>Integrated expense splitting within the trip flow</li><li>Real-time changes: everyone’s on the same page</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Explains Wanderlog’s strengths and its gaps for group-focused trips.">
  <h2>How Does Wanderlog Compare for Planning a Trip with Friends?</h2>
  <p data-citations="wanderlog-plan-trip,wanderlog-android">Wanderlog is strong for building detailed itineraries, viewing routes on maps, and managing travel reservations. It offers group collaboration, so friends can comment or edit plans. However, Wanderlog is not built around group decision points: there’s no structured date poll or integrated expense splitting. Organisers using Wanderlog often end up running date votes in a separate group chat and shifting to Splitwise or other apps to handle money.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-wanderlog-plan-trip">[wanderlog-plan-trip]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-wanderlog-android">[wanderlog-android]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Detailed itinerary and map-based route planning</li><li>Group collaboration for itinerary editing and commenting</li><li>No built-in solution for date polling</li><li>No expense splitting—requires an extra tool</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Highlights Splitwise’s usefulness for expenses—and its limits for group trip planning.">
  <h2>What About Splitwise as a Trip Planner?</h2>
  <p data-citations="splitwise-home">Splitwise is effective for tracking who owes whom after a trip. For group travel, you can create a group and record expenses as you go—but Splitwise isn’t a planner. It doesn’t help you poll for dates, coordinate an itinerary, or replace the group chat. Most organisers using Splitwise still have to chase people for decisions in another app.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-splitwise-home">[splitwise-home]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Great for splitting expenses at the end or during the trip</li><li>No support for itinerary building</li><li>No date coordination—relies on external tools</li><li>Settling balances is separated from planning steps</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Examines why multipurpose tools break down for complex group trips.">
  <h2>Are Google Sheets or WhatsApp Enough for Group Trip Planning?</h2>
  <p data-citations="google-sheets,tryps-homepage">Google Sheets gives flexibility: you can make your own poll, itinerary table, and expense columns. But Sheets requires everyone to engage, and someone to push updates and police the data. WhatsApp keeps things fast, but trip plans get buried among memes and side talk. For organisers, these tools work—until someone misses key info or you’re stuck reconciling three versions.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-google-sheets">[google-sheets]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Sheets: flexible but fragmented; easy to miss votes or expenses</li><li>WhatsApp: easy for chat, poor for capturing group decisions</li><li>Organiser does most of the follow-up manually</li><li>No built-in structure for group consensus</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Discusses TripIt’s role and its fit for collaborative planning.">
  <h2>How Does TripIt Fit in Group Trip Planning?</h2>
  <p data-citations="tripit-how-it-works,tripit-getting-started">TripIt shines at making sense of your travel bookings. Forward confirmation emails or let it sync with your inbox, and TripIt builds a single itinerary. You can share this with friends, but collaborative planning—choosing dates, editing plans, or tracking shared costs—is out of scope. TripIt works best for one-way trip organisation, not two-way group coordination.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tripit-how-it-works">[tripit-how-it-works]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tripit-getting-started">[tripit-getting-started]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>Automatic travel itinerary creation from emails</li><li>Easily shareable with others</li><li>No built-in tool for group voting, itinerary editing, or expense splitting</li><li>Best as a travel organiser, not as a planner for groups</li></ul>
</section>
<section data-purpose="Provides a straight comparison for common organiser workflows.">
  <h2>TRYPS vs Wanderlog vs Splitwise—Which Should You Use?</h2>
  <p data-citations="tryps-homepage,wanderlog-plan-trip,splitwise-home">If you need to herd cats—get your group to choose dates, agree on what to do, and settle up—TRYPS is built for you. Wanderlog is a fit if your group already has dates and just wants to share ideas for what to do. Splitwise is best reserved for settling who owes what, once the trip is over. If you’re tired of switching between three apps and endless chats, TRYPS consolidates everything in a workflow your friends can’t ignore.<span class="citations"><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-tryps-homepage">[tryps-homepage]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-wanderlog-plan-trip">[wanderlog-plan-trip]</a></sup><sup class="citation"><a href="#source-splitwise-home">[splitwise-home]</a></sup></span></p>
  <ul><li>TRYPS: end-to-end group planning—date poll, itinerary, and expenses</li><li>Wanderlog: focus on planning routes and activities</li><li>Splitwise: only for tracking and clearing expenses</li></ul>
</section>
